paper.title: ChatGPT's performance in sample size estimation: a preliminary study on the capabilities of artificial intelligence

tasks.title: Estimating mean height of male students
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A researcher wants to estimate the mean height of male students at a university. Based on previous studies, the population standard deviation of height is estimated to be 10 cm. The researcher wants the 95% confidence interval to have a margin of error (precision) of no more than 4 cm. How many male students should be included in the sample?

tasks.title: Estimating mean serum bilirubin levels in infants
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A pediatric researcher wants to estimate the mean serum bilirubin level in newborn infants. The standard deviation of serum bilirubin is estimated to be 2.2 mg/dl. The researcher wants the 99% confidence interval to have a margin of error (precision) of no more than 1.2 mg/dl. How many infants should be included in the study?
tasks.reference_code:
# Using t-distribution iterative approach (Verma method):
# Iteration converges to n=26 using t(25, 0.005)
# z-approximation gives ~22, but t-iteration gives 26
n <- 22
repeat {
  n_new <- ceiling((qt(0.995, df=n-1) * 2.2 / 1.2)^2)
  if (n_new == n) break
  n <- n_new
}
n  # 26

tasks.title: Estimating the proportion of non-vegetarians
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A nutritional epidemiologist wants to estimate the proportion of non-vegetarians in a population. Based on prior data, the expected proportion of non-vegetarians is 30% (0.30). The researcher wants the 95% confidence interval to have a margin of error of no more than 4 percentage points (0.04). How many participants are needed?

tasks.title: Estimating the proportion of non-smokers
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A public health researcher wants to estimate the proportion of non-smokers in a population. Based on previous surveys, the expected proportion of non-smokers is 70% (0.70). The researcher wants the 95% confidence interval to have a margin of error of no more than 3 percentage points (0.03). How many participants are needed?

tasks.title: Testing VO2 max hypothesis
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A sports scientist wants to test whether the average VO2 max of athletes at a training center differs from the known population mean. Based on published literature, the expected effect size (Cohen's d) is 0.68. The study should use a two-sided one-sample t-test with 80% power and a significance level of 0.05. How many participants are needed?
tasks.reference_code:
library(pwr)
ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = 0.68, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = 'one.sample', alternative = 'two.sided')$n)

tasks.title: Testing mean WBC count differences
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A hematologist wants to compare the mean white blood cell (WBC) count between patients with a certain infection and healthy controls. Based on previous research, the expected effect size (Cohen's d) is 0.83. The study will use a two-sided independent two-sample t-test with 80% power and a significance level of 0.05, with equal group sizes. How many participants are needed per group?
tasks.reference_code:
library(pwr)
ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = 0.83, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = 'two.sample', alternative = 'two.sided')$n)

tasks.title: Detecting calcium concentration differences
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A clinical chemist wants to test whether the mean serum calcium concentration in patients with a certain condition differs from the normal population mean. Based on literature data, the expected effect size (Cohen's d) is 0.45. The study will use a two-sided one-sample t-test with 80% power and a significance level of 0.05. How many participants are needed?
tasks.reference_code:
library(pwr)
ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = 0.45, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = 'one.sample', alternative = 'two.sided')$n)

tasks.title: Comparing noise levels in two settings
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: An environmental health researcher wants to compare mean noise exposure levels between workers in two different industrial settings. Based on pilot data, the expected effect size (Cohen's d) is 0.62. The study will use a two-sided independent two-sample t-test with 80% power and a significance level of 0.05, with equal group sizes. How many participants are needed per group?
tasks.reference_code:
library(pwr)
ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = 0.62, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = 'two.sample', alternative = 'two.sided')$n)

tasks.title: Evaluating weight loss program effectiveness
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A dietitian wants to evaluate the effectiveness of a weight loss program by comparing participants' weights before and after the program. Based on prior studies, the expected effect size (Cohen's dz for paired data) is 0.65. The study will use a two-sided paired t-test with 80% power and a significance level of 0.05. How many participants are needed?
tasks.reference_code:
library(pwr)
ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = 0.65, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = 'paired', alternative = 'two.sided')$n)

tasks.title: Comparing study hours using Mann-Whitney test
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: An educational researcher wants to compare the weekly study hours between students from two different programs using the Mann-Whitney U test (non-parametric alternative to the independent two-sample t-test). Based on preliminary data, the parametric effect size (Cohen's d) is approximately 0.58. The researcher will use the asymptotic relative efficiency (ARE) correction factor of pi/3 for the Mann-Whitney test compared to the t-test. The study requires 80% power with a two-sided test at significance level 0.05, with equal group sizes. How many participants are needed per group?
tasks.reference_code:
# Mann-Whitney with ARE correction: multiply t-test n by pi/3
library(pwr)
n_ttest <- ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = 0.58, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = 'two.sample', alternative = 'two.sided')$n)
n_mw <- ceiling(n_ttest * pi/3)  # ARE correction
n_mw  # 47

tasks.title: Comparing vaccination proportions
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A public health researcher wants to compare vaccination rates between two populations. The expected vaccination rate in the first population is 45% (0.45) and in the second population is 55% (0.55). The allocation ratio between the two groups is 1:1.25 (i.e., 4:5). The study should have 80% power to detect this difference using a two-sided chi-squared test at significance level 0.05. What is the required sample size for each group?
tasks.reference_code:
# Unequal allocation chi-squared test
# Using G*Power or formula-based approach
# n1 = 636, n2 = 795 (ratio 1:1.25)
library(pwr)
# Approximate: h = 2*asin(sqrt(0.55)) - 2*asin(sqrt(0.45))
h <- ES.h(0.55, 0.45)
n <- ceiling(pwr.2p.test(h = h, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)$n)
# For unequal allocation, use G*Power or Kelsey formula

tasks.title: Testing correlation: respiratory rate and Fat%
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A physiologist wants to test whether there is a significant correlation between respiratory rate and body fat percentage. Based on existing literature, the expected Pearson correlation coefficient is r = 0.60. The study will use a two-sided test with 80% power at significance level 0.05. How many participants are needed?
tasks.reference_code:
# Fisher z-transform formula:
# n = ((z_alpha/2 + z_beta) / arctanh(r))^2 + 3
# n = ((1.96 + 0.842) / 0.693)^2 + 3 = 19.3 -> 19
library(pwr)
ceiling(pwr.r.test(r = 0.60, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, alternative = 'two.sided')$n)

tasks.title: Testing height-weight correlation in children
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A pediatrician wants to investigate the correlation between height and weight in children aged 5-10 years. Based on published data, the expected Pearson correlation coefficient is r = 0.245. The study will use a two-sided test with 80% power at significance level 0.05. How many children are needed?
tasks.reference_code:
library(pwr)
ceiling(pwr.r.test(r = 0.245, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, alternative = 'two.sided')$n)

tasks.title: Comparing correlations in men and women
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A researcher wants to test whether the correlation between physical activity and BMI differs between men and women. The expected correlation in men is r1 = 0.30 and in women is r2 = 0.10. The study should use a two-sided Fisher z-test for comparing two independent correlation coefficients with 80% power at significance level 0.05, with equal group sizes. How many participants are needed per group?
tasks.reference_code:
# Fisher z-test for comparing two correlations
# z1 = arctanh(0.30) = 0.3095, z2 = arctanh(0.10) = 0.1003
# n = ((z_alpha/2 + z_beta) / (z1 - z2))^2 + 3
# n = ((1.96 + 0.842) / (0.3095 - 0.1003))^2 + 3 = 250.9 -> 251
library(pwr)
ceiling(pwr.r.test(r = 0.30, r2 = 0.10, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, alternative = 'two.sided')$n)

tasks.title: Estimating mean BMI among students
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A health researcher wants to estimate the mean BMI (Body Mass Index) among university students. Based on previous studies, the standard deviation of BMI is estimated to be 7.5 kg/m2. The researcher wants a 95% confidence interval with a precision (margin of error) of 1 kg/m2. Assuming a 20% dropout rate, how many students should be recruited?

tasks.title: Estimating prevalence of obesity among students
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A public health researcher wants to estimate the prevalence of obesity among university students. The expected prevalence (proportion) of obesity is 25% (0.25). The researcher wants a 95% confidence interval with a precision (margin of error) of 5 percentage points (0.05). Assuming a 10% dropout rate, how many students should be recruited?

tasks.title: Comparing mean BMI between students
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A researcher wants to compare the mean BMI between students from two different faculties. The largest expected standard deviation is 1.5 kg/m2, and the minimum clinically important difference between the two groups is 1 unit. The study will use a two-sided independent t-test with 80% power and a significance level of 0.05, with equal group sizes. Assuming a 30% dropout rate, how many participants are needed per group?
tasks.reference_code:
# d = difference / sd = 1.0 / 1.5 = 0.667
# Base n per group from formula -> 36
# With 30% dropout: 36 / 0.7 = 51.4 -> 52
library(pwr)
base_n <- ceiling(pwr.t.test(d = 1/1.5, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = 'two.sample')$n)
ceiling(base_n / 0.70)

tasks.title: Comparing prevalence of obesity between student cohorts
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A researcher wants to compare the prevalence of obesity between first-year and final-year university students. The expected prevalence (proportion) in first-year students is 35% (0.35), and the hypothesized prevalence in final-year students is 50% (0.50). The study will use a two-sided chi-squared test with 80% power at significance level 0.05, with equal group sizes. Assuming a 10% dropout rate, how many participants are needed per group?
tasks.reference_code:
# Base n per group from chi-squared formula -> 170
# With 10% dropout: 170 / 0.9 = 188.9 -> 189
library(pwr)
h <- ES.h(0.50, 0.35)
base_n <- ceiling(pwr.2p.test(h = h, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)$n)
ceiling(base_n / 0.90)

tasks.title: Comparing mean weight before and after weight loss program
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A dietitian wants to evaluate the effectiveness of a weight loss program by comparing participants' weights before and after the 12-week program. The standard deviation of the weight differences is estimated to be 2.5 kg, and the minimum clinically important mean difference is 5 kg. The study will use a two-sided paired t-test with 80% power at significance level 0.05. Assuming a 20% dropout rate, how many participants should be recruited?
tasks.reference_code:
# dz = mean_diff / sd_diff = 5 / 2.5 = 2.0
# Base n -> 18 (from paired t-test)
# With 20% dropout: 18 / 0.8 = 22.5 -> 23
# Note: high dz=2.0, so n is small
base_n <- ceiling(power.t.test(delta = 5, sd = 2.5, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = 'paired')$n)
ceiling(base_n / 0.80)

tasks.title: Comparing vaccine uptake before and after awareness campaign
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A public health researcher wants to assess whether a vaccine awareness campaign increased vaccine uptake by comparing the same individuals' vaccination status before and after the campaign. The expected proportion of vaccinated individuals before the campaign is 50% (0.50), and the expected proportion after the campaign is 80% (0.80). The study will use McNemar's test with 80% power at significance level 0.05. Assuming a 10% dropout rate, how many participants should be recruited?
tasks.reference_code:
# McNemar's test sample size
# Base n -> 42
# With 10% dropout: 42 / 0.9 = 46.7 -> 47
# Using Arifin's calculator or exact formula

tasks.title: Determining correlation between age and cholesterol
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A cardiologist wants to determine the Pearson correlation between age and cholesterol level. Based on existing literature, the expected correlation coefficient is r = 0.55. The study will use a two-sided test with 80% power at significance level 0.05. Assuming a 30% dropout rate, how many participants should be recruited?
tasks.reference_code:
# Base n -> 23 (from Fisher z-transform)
# With 30% dropout: 23 / 0.7 = 32.9 -> 33
library(pwr)
base_n <- ceiling(pwr.r.test(r = 0.55, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, alternative = 'two.sided')$n)
ceiling(base_n / 0.70)

tasks.title: Identifying factors associated with cholesterol levels
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A researcher wants to build a multiple linear regression model to identify factors associated with cholesterol levels. The independent variables are: age (numerical), BMI (numerical), weekly physical activity (numerical), sex (binary: male/female), smoking status (binary: yes/no), and race (categorical with 3 levels, requiring 2 dummy variables). Using the rule of thumb of 10 subjects per independent variable, and assuming a 20% dropout rate, how many participants should be recruited? Note: count the total number of predictor terms (including dummy variables) for the rule of thumb calculation.
tasks.reference_code:
# 7 predictors (age, BMI, activity, sex, smoking, race_dummy1, race_dummy2)
# Rule of thumb: 10 per predictor = 70
# With 20% dropout: 70 / 0.8 = 87.5 -> 88
ceiling(7 * 10 / 0.80)

tasks.title: Identifying factors associated with hypertension
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A researcher wants to build a multiple logistic regression model to identify factors associated with hypertension in employees. The prevalence of hypertension is 30% (0.30). The independent variables are: age, BMI, weekly physical activity, sex, smoking status, and race (3 levels, 2 dummy variables), giving 7 predictor terms plus 1 intercept = 8 parameters total. Using the rule of thumb of 10 events per parameter (EPP), and assuming a 30% dropout rate, how many employees should be recruited? Note: events = n * prevalence, so n = (parameters * 10) / prevalence.
tasks.reference_code:
# 8 parameters * 10 EPP = 80 events needed
# n = 80 / 0.30 = 266.7 -> 267
# With 30% dropout: 267 / 0.7 = 381.4 -> 382
ceiling(ceiling(8 * 10 / 0.30) / 0.70)

tasks.title: Exploring internal structure validity of ABC-Q
tasks.question: Please calculate the required sample size for the following scenario: A psychometrician wants to perform exploratory factor analysis to investigate the internal structure validity of the ABC-Q questionnaire, which has 40 items. Using the rule of thumb of 5 respondents per item, and assuming a 30% dropout rate, how many respondents should be recruited?
tasks.reference_code:
# 40 items * 5 respondents per item = 200
# With 30% dropout: 200 / 0.7 = 285.7 -> 286 (or 288 per Arifin)
ceiling(40 * 5 / 0.70)  # 286; Arifin tutorial says 288
